State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         323-6444

August 25, 1980


Doug Carter
Post Office Box 7600
Stockton, CA 95207

Dear Doug:

Thank you for your letter of August 2, 1980, regarding
a mailing sent out by your opponents. While the Political
Reform Act does regulate identification of mass mailing
(see Section 84305), it does not prohibit the half screen-
ing which appeared on the literature you enclosed. However,
Section 29410 (enclosed) of the Elections Code does pro-
hibit half screened identification. As you know, our
agency does not regulate or investigate sections of the
Elections Code.

For further information on this subject you may wish
to contact your local authorities. If we can be of any
assistance in matters dealing with the Political Reform
Act, a copy of which is enclosed, please do not hesitate
to contact us.

Sincerely,

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

RMS:nc

Enclosures

DOUG CARTER
P.O. BOX 7600
STOCKTON, CA.95207

AUG 6 0 52 AH 60

Dear Mr. Huston—

After reading the August California
Journal, I thought I'd bring this matter (enclosed) to
your attention.

There were 3 Co. Chairman of [illegible]
Reagan effort. Two of the work for me. They
were both amazed at the Fed U-Sherman's letter to
just one to Kiernan. Mrs Carter Dunlap's called Kegan's
L.A. Headquarters May 27 & was re-awarded in this
was kingldon against some Reagan's name. The letter
arrived "2 and 3 step later in the district & we was pyre of
came out argument. No one saw the original except
I hope you will send something to regule Kegan
identifaction of me receiving. [illegible] by Adrian Endie's is
Campaign Manager Ray McLeod & the form of hopefully all
[illegible]

Doug Carter

Marti Nielsen
San Joaquin County Co-Chairman
Reagan for President Committee

Cliff Koster
Immediate Past President
San Joaquin County Farm Bureau

May 26, 1980

Dear Fellow Republican:

For the fifth time in eight years, Doug Carter is running for the State
Assembly -- and this time, we must strongly oppose his nomination on June 3.

Here's why:

* Doug Carter cannot win in November. He ran for Assembly in 1974, and
lost. He ran in 1976, and lost. He ran in 1978, lost and promised never to
run again. But now he is back -- and he will lead Republicans to defeat
again in 1980, unless we stop him.

* Doug Carter's old campaign organization is deeply in debt. Since 1974,
Doug Carter has spent over $250,000 on his losing political battles. Of that
amount, he still owes over $60,000.

* Doug Carter was a one-time Assemblyman for just 17 short months back in
1973 -- but according to the respected California Journal, he was a "maverick"
who often sided with liberal Democrats from Los Angeles and San Francisco
... and voted against his Republican colleagues 42 percent of the time.
Official records also show that he failed to vote on over 450 critically
important measures ranging from tax relief to health care for the elderly.

* In July 1974, Doug Carter embarrassed Governor Ronald Reagan by accusing
him of "patronage politics" ... and then by leading an Assembly fight to defeat
a veto by Governor Reagan -- a veto designed to save taxpayers over $10 million.

* Although Doug Carter now claims to be a "fiscal conservative," official
Assembly records show he was actually an advocate of bigger government budgets
and more spending. One Carter bill, for example, would have cost taxpayers an
extra $10 million (AB 4504).

As you can see, we Republicans can no longer afford to support Doug Carter.
He has embarrassed Governor Reagan and other Republican leaders. He has fought
against Republicans in the State Assembly. And he has led our party to defeat
year ... after year ... after year.

On June 3, join us in rejecting Doug Carter's long history of political
defeat -- and help us put the Republican Party back on a winning track.

Marti Nielsen                                Clifford W. Koster

P.S. Remember Doug Carter's bitter fight with Governor Reagan?
The enclosed newspaper article tells the whole story.

Tracy Press                                                                                                                          March 26, 1980

Doug Carter returns
By Earl G. Waters

SACRAMENTO — It is now con-
firmed that Republican Doug Carter
(no kin to the President) will again seek
the 26th Assembly District seat which
represents parts of San Joaquin and
Stanislaus counties. For the millions
who have never heard of Doug Carter,
he is rapidly becoming the "Harold
he is rapidly becoming the "Harold
Stassen" of the legislative jousts.

A former hockey player, Carter got
his feet wet in politics serving as an
aide to former Assemblyman Robert
Monagan. Although that relationship
was severed under mysterious circum-
stances, modesty did not prevent
Carter from claiming to be "the only
candidate with legislative experience"
when he ran for that seat to replace
Monagan who resigned for a federal
appointment.

Elected, he had an immediate seiz-
ure of "feeling his oats." Barely in
office he demanded that Governor
Ronald Reagan recognize his awesome
power by filling a vacancy on the San
Joaquin County Board of Supervisors
with a candidate of his choice. He put
this in an ultimatum. If Reagan didn't
appoint his man he wouldn't run for
re-election. That was in 1974. Obvi-
ously the governor concluded the
legislature and the Republican Party
could survive without Carter, for he
appointed someone else, and the
angered Carter promptly announced
his withdrawal from the assembly
race.

Having acted in haste, Carter later
repented and attempted to get back
into the contest. He was soundly
trounced by John Garamendi who de-
feated him 2 to 1.

Undaunted, Carter ran again in 1976
and again in 1978, receiving fewer
votes the last time than the time be-

fore. Now he advises in an eight page
"news release" that he will again be a
candidate. He is young enough to con-
tinue until he has completely dwarfed
Stassen's record as a continuous office
seeker.

He says he is motivated by "duty,
loyalty to friends, and dedication," yet
it can be suspected that the $28,000
salary plus the $1,500 a month tax free
"living expenses" along with a free
automobile, gasoline and a plush pen-
sion at the end of the line, form a
powerful incentive for his perennial
campaigning.

The intervening years with their de-
feats at the polls have not served to
humble Carter. With no inhibitions he
lays claim to having invented the de-
terminate sentence legislation, as well
as the indexing of income taxes and the
repeal of the business inventory tax.

And, with reapportionment as an
upcoming issue following the census,
Carter declares, "I am the only candi-
date who understands the nuances of
reapportionment, and has the strength
of character, experience and intellig-
ence to fight effectively for our inter-
ests." He adds that "political candi-
dates are mostly glad-handing empty
heads."

He intimates support from his
church, the Lion's Club, Masons,
Grange and Farm Bureau and lays all
out claim to Republican Party support
including that of Ronald Reagan. This,
despite the fact that in the 1978 primary
election the combined vote of the two
Republican candidates running against
him was greater than the vote he re-
ceived and he received fewer votes in
the general election than he had two
years earlier.

sections.question_synthetic: Does the Political Reform Act prohibit half-screened identification on mass mailings, and what regulations apply to campaign literature identification requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: While the Political Reform Act regulates mass mailing identification under Section 84305, it does not prohibit half-screening. However, Section 29410 of the Elections Code does prohibit half-screened identification, but that statute falls outside FPPC jurisdiction.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a formal FPPC advice letter response dated August 25, 1980. No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present, but letter contains FPPC's legal determination. Supporting documentation includes Carter's original complaint letter and the campaign mailer in question. Letter clarifies jurisdictional boundaries between Political Reform Act and Elections Code.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Political Reform Act prohibit half-screened identification on mass mailings, and what regulations apply to campaign literature identification requirements?

CONCLUSION: While the Political Reform Act regulates mass mailing identification under Section 84305, it does not prohibit half-screening. However, Section 29410 of the Elections Code does prohibit half-screened identification, but that statute falls outside FPPC jurisdiction.
embedding.first_500_words: August 25, 1980 Doug Carter Post Office Box 7600 Stockton, CA 95207 Dear Doug: Thank you for your letter of August 2, 1980, regarding a mailing sent out by your opponents. While the Political Reform Act does regulate identification of mass mailings (see Section 84305), it does not prohibit the half screening which appeared on the literature you enclosed. However, Section 29410 (enclosed) of the Elections Code does prohibit half screened identification. As you know, our agency does not regulate or investigate sections of the Elections Code. For further information on this subject you may wish to contact your local authorities. If we can be of any assistance in matters dealing with the Political Reform Act, a copy of which is enclosed, please do not hesitate to contact us. Sincerely, [Signature] Robert M. Stern General Counsel RMS:nc Enclosures DOUG CARTER P.O. BOX 7600 STOCKTON, CA. 95207 Dear Mr. Houston, After reading the August California Journal, I thought I'd bring this matter (enclosed) to your attention. There were 3 Co-Chairmen of our county Reagan effort. Two of the three were for me. They were both unaware of the third Co-Chairman's letter. In fact, one Co-Chairman, Mrs. Carter Dunlap, called Reagan's L.A. Headquarters May 27 & was re-assured nothing was being done against me in Reagan's name. The letter arrived 2 and 3 days later in the district & was very effective. To this day, people ask me why Reagan's committee came out against me. No one saw the screened 6 point type. I hope you will do something to require larger identification & no screening. It was done by Adrian Forder, As Campaign manager Gary McCornell & the firm of Hackley & McNally. Sincerely, Doug Carter Dear Fellow Republican: For the fifth time in eight years, Doug Carter is running for the State Assembly -- and this time, we must strongly oppose his nomination on June 3. Here's why: * Doug Carter cannot win in November. He ran for Assembly in 1974, and lost. He ran in 1976, and lost. He ran in 1978, lost and promised never to run again. But now he is back -- and he will lead Republicans to defeat again in 1980, unless we stop him. * Doug Carter's old campaign organization is deeply in debt. Since 1974, Doug Carter has spent over $250,000 on his losing political battles. Of that amount, he still owes over $60,000. * Doug Carter was a one-time Assemblyman for just 17 short months back in 1973 -- but according to the respected California Journal, he was a "maverick" who often sided with liberal Democrats from Los Angeles and San Francisco ... and voted against his Republican colleagues 42 percent of the time. embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Robert Stern responds to Doug Carter's complaint about a half-screened campaign mailing opposing his candidacy, explaining that while the Political Reform Act regulates mass mailing identification, it does not prohibit half-screening, though the Elections Code does prohibit this practice.
